visual_checks: CRITICAL — TABLE ROLE IDENTIFICATION: For every table in the document, determine whether it is a data table (presenting information in rows and columns where headers give context to the data) or a layout table (used only to position content visually).
For each data table, identify the header row(s) and/or header column(s). Are they visually distinct (bold, shaded, different background)?

Check whether header cells are programmatically identified: in PDFs, look for <TH> tags in the tag tree; in DOCX, check if 'Header Row' is enabled in Table Properties; in PPTX, check table style options.
Look for complex tables with merged cells, multi-level headers, or split cells — these are especially prone to accessibility failures.
Check whether tables span multiple pages and whether headers repeat on each page (important for long data tables in PDF/DOCX).
Identify any layout tables that are used to arrange content side-by-side rather than present data. These should not have header markup.
Look for nested tables (tables within tables) which create significant barriers for screen reader navigation.
pass_conditions: All data tables have header rows and/or header columns programmatically identified using the correct mechanism for the file format
In PDFs, data table headers use <TH> tags with appropriate scope attributes, and data cells use <TD> tags within a proper <Table> > <TR> structure
In DOCX, the 'Header Row' option is checked in Table Properties and/or the first row uses a header style
Layout tables are marked as layout/decorative (artifact in PDF) so screen readers do not announce them as data tables
Complex tables with merged cells have correct header associations so each data cell can be linked to its row and column headers
Long tables that span multiple pages repeat the header row on each page
fail_conditions: Data tables have no header row or column identified — all cells are tagged as <TD> or data cells with no <TH> designation
Tables have a visual header row (bold, shaded) but it is not programmatically marked as a header
In PDFs, the table structure in the tag tree is missing or incorrect (e.g., no <Table>, <TR>, <TH>, <TD> tags)
Layout tables are tagged as data tables with header markup, causing screen readers to announce grid navigation for non-tabular content
Complex tables with merged cells have broken header-to-data cell associations
Tables are created using tabs or spaces to align columns visually rather than using actual table elements
Nested tables make it impossible for screen readers to determine relationships between headers and data
na_conditions: Document contains no tables of any kind
Document contains only decorative borders or lines that might look like table elements but are not tables
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Document could not be opened or its table structure could not be inspected
Tables are too complex to determine header associations from the available information
common_mistakes: Creating a visual header row with bold text and shading but not enabling the 'Header Row' property in Word or marking cells as <TH> in the PDF tag tree
Using tab characters or spaces to create the visual appearance of a table instead of inserting an actual table
Merging cells in complex ways that break the row/column relationship, making it impossible for assistive technology to associate data with headers
Not marking layout tables as artifacts/decorative, so screen readers announce 'table with 3 columns and 5 rows' for content that is not tabular
Applying table headers only to the first row when the table also has a header column on the left side
Forgetting to repeat header rows on multi-page tables so users lose context on the second and subsequent pages

examples.pass: A PDF contains a budget table. The first row with 'Category', 'Q1', 'Q2', 'Q3', 'Q4' is tagged with <TH> cells with scope='column'. A screen reader navigating to cell '$5,000' announces 'Q2, Office Supplies, $5,000' providing full context.
examples.fail: A Word document has a 10-row table listing employee names, departments, and phone numbers. The first row is visually bold and shaded gray but 'Header Row' is not enabled. A screen reader reads each cell without announcing the column name, so the user hears '$45,000' without knowing it refers to 'Salary'.
examples.partial: A PDF has 4 data tables. Two have proper <TH> tags, but the other two have all cells as <TD> with no header identification despite having visually distinct header rows.
